meta.title: Social Medicine & Public Health — Past Papers

behaviour.mcq: Select one option. Answer revealed immediately on selection.

questions.stem: Disadvantages of spermicides / chemical contraceptives:

options.C: They do not protect against venereal diseases

questions.explanation: The key limitation of spermicides is that they give no protection against STIs/venereal diseases (unlike condoms).

questions.stem: Psychology of change (in improving healthcare quality) — which is true?

options.A: Adopting a clearly improved idea rarely meets obstacles
options.B: Improvement is achieved when the entire population implements the change
options.D: In a few groups ~50% delay adopting a clearly improved idea

questions.explanation: Per Rogers' diffusion curve, innovation does meet resistance, full adoption is not required, and resisters (laggards) are ~16%, not 50% — so all three specific statements are wrong.

questions.stem: Regarding the Bradford-Hill criteria, which is correct?

options.A: There are 9, including "strength of association" and "plausibility"

questions.explanation: Bradford-Hill = 9 criteria (strength, consistency, specificity, temporality, biological gradient, plausibility, coherence, experiment, analogy). "Prevalence" and "reusability" are not criteria.

questions.stem: Modifiable risk factors for Coronary Artery Disease are:
questions.explanation: Dyslipidemia is modifiable. Gender & family history are non-modifiable; low BMI is not a CHD risk factor.

questions.stem: The onset of an infectious disease requires:

options.A: The absence of a susceptible host
options.D: The presence of a causal factor

questions.explanation: Disease needs a causative agent (plus a susceptible host and a route of transmission) — the chain of infection.

questions.stem: Meta-analysis is:

options.B: The analysis of only interventional studies
options.C: The quantitative synthesis of results from individual studies
options.D: The qualitative synthesis of results from individual studies

questions.explanation: Meta-analysis = quantitative synthesis of results across individual studies (a weighted pooled estimate).

questions.stem: Which measure of association is estimated in a case-control study?
questions.explanation: Case-control studies yield the odds ratio (they cannot directly measure incidence/relative risk).

questions.stem: In secondary prevention of breast cancer, all are true EXCEPT:

options.A: MRI in women with a family history of breast cancer

questions.explanation: Prophylactic mastectomy = primary prevention (removing the organ before disease), so it is NOT part of secondary prevention/screening.

questions.stem: Regarding improvement of quality in healthcare, which is NOT true?

options.A: Related processes run parallel to and connected with daily clinical practice
options.C: Continuous, combined efforts of all involved professionals are necessary
options.D: Adoption of necessary changes to improve therapeutic outcomes

questions.explanation: The other three statements are all valid QI principles, so "All are wrong" is itself the untrue option.

questions.stem: Measurement of disease frequency can be done with:
questions.explanation: Prevalence (and incidence) measure disease frequency. Odds ratio & relative risk are measures of association.

questions.stem: "Countries with wet habits" regarding alcohol means:

options.A: Countries producing wine with few dependency cases
options.B: Countries producing alcohol that border the sea
options.C: Countries with high alcohol abuse and intoxication
options.D: Countries with large surface water that produce alcohol

questions.explanation: "Wet" drinking cultures = high alcohol abuse and intoxication patterns.

questions.stem: Regarding the goals of Health Education:

options.C: They presuppose the process of decision-making and behaviour change
options.D: Not always specific, as long as they include awareness, knowledge, value prioritization

questions.explanation: Health-education objectives presuppose decision-making and behaviour change (targeting knowledge → attitudes → behaviour).

questions.stem: Rubella is transmitted:

options.A: After injury with a contaminated object
options.D: By direct contact with sufferers through droplets

questions.explanation: Rubella spreads by direct droplet contact (respiratory route).

questions.stem: Accidents in developed countries, by frequency/cause of death, rank:

options.A: 3rd, after circulatory diseases and malignant neoplasms
options.C: 4th, after malignant disease, substance abuse and alcohol

questions.explanation: In developed countries injuries/accidents are typically the 3rd leading cause of death, after cardiovascular disease and cancer.

questions.stem: WHO's goal: reduce the life-expectancy gap between socioeconomic groups by at least:
questions.explanation: The WHO target is a reduction of at least 25%.

questions.stem: Maximum blood nicotine concentration is achieved within:
questions.explanation: Peak blood nicotine is reached within about 10 minutes.

questions.stem: WHO goals for the 21st century include all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Increasing fertility is not a WHO goal; the goals target reducing communicable & non-communicable disease and healthy ageing.

questions.stem: In direct transmission of infectious diseases:

options.A: Host and susceptible host are more than 1.5 m apart
options.B: Host and susceptible host are less than 1.5 m apart
options.C: Transmission occurs only through contact between two people

questions.explanation: Direct (droplet) transmission occurs at close range — typically < 1.5 m.

questions.stem: Which is true regarding early historical observations?

options.A: Treatment of scurvy symptoms by consuming fruits and vegetables
options.B: Thicker skulls of Egyptians (vs Persians) from enriched clothing
options.C: Treatment of night blindness by consuming meat with honey
options.D: Lower prevalence of osteomalacia in areas with greater sunlight

questions.explanation: The classic early observation is that fruit/vegetables cure scurvy (Lind, vitamin C). Note: lower osteomalacia in sunny areas (vitamin D) is also a valid historical observation, but the Egyptian-skull and night-blindness options are stated incorrectly.

questions.stem: The second phase of demographic maturity is characterized by:

options.C: High birth rate and decreasing mortality
options.D: Slow decrease in birth rate with a significant decrease in mortality

questions.explanation: Phase 2 (early expanding) = high fertility with falling mortality → rapid population growth.

questions.stem: The types of receptors / reservoirs are:

options.D: Four: humans, animals, environment and microbes

questions.explanation: Reservoirs are classified as Human, Animal and Environmental — three types.

questions.stem: "Countries with wet habits" regarding alcohol means:

options.A: Countries producing alcohol that are wet from the sea
options.B: Countries with high alcohol abuse and intoxication
options.C: Countries with large surface waters that produce alcohol
options.D: Countries producing wine with few dependency cases

questions.explanation: "Wet" cultures = high alcohol abuse and intoxication.

questions.stem: Which of the following is true?

options.A: Secondary prevention aims to inform and sensitise increased-risk groups
options.C: Primary prevention concerns the entire population, healthy and sick
options.D: Tertiary prevention aims at rehabilitation, adaptation and prevention of relapse

questions.explanation: Each statement is defensible in the course's framing (secondary→high-risk outreach/screening, primary→whole population, tertiary→rehabilitation) → All are correct.

questions.stem: Life expectancy is:

options.A: Higher in infants regardless of gender

questions.explanation: Life expectancy is generally higher in women.

questions.stem: Based on the relationship between economy and health:

options.A: Poverty doesn't contribute to disease (no meat/sugar consumed)
options.C: Poverty contributes to the manifestation of disease

questions.explanation: Poverty contributes to disease — a core social determinant of health.

questions.stem: Maximum blood nicotine concentration is achieved within:
questions.explanation: About 10 minutes.

questions.stem: Healthy life expectancy is an indicator that answers questions such as:

options.A: Which risk factor is most dangerous for health?
options.B: Does improving health also come with longevity?
options.C: At which prevention level can we intervene?
options.D: Which disease is responsible for most deaths?

questions.explanation: HALE addresses whether improvements in health are accompanied by longevity (living longer in good health).

questions.stem: A risk factor for cancer in the adult Greek population is:
questions.explanation: Obesity is a modifiable cancer risk factor; the others are protective.

questions.stem: Which is NOT true about hepatitis B vaccination?

options.A: It is completed in one dose
options.B: It is the main prevention against HBV
options.C: Recommended for all HCWs not vaccinated in childhood
options.D: One of the mandatory childhood vaccinations

questions.explanation: The HBV vaccine is a multi-dose (3-dose) schedule, not one dose.

questions.stem: An advantage of the prospective cohort study is that:

options.A: It can calculate all confounding factors
options.B: It can only calculate relative risks
options.C: It can only calculate odds ratios

questions.explanation: Cohort studies follow disease-free people forward, so they can measure incidence (and relative risk).

questions.stem: The second phase of demographic transition is characterized by:

options.D: Slow reduction in fertility with significant reduction in mortality

questions.explanation: Phase 2 = high fertility, falling mortality → rapid growth.

questions.stem: The statistical model for estimating risk from CVD (SCORE) includes:
questions.explanation: SCORE uses age, sex, smoking, cholesterol and systolic BP (hypertension).

questions.stem: Epidemiological surveillance of smallpox includes:

options.A: Chemoprophylaxis of health personnel during an outbreak
options.B: Disinfection of patients' faeces and urine
options.D: Strict isolation when the patient is hospitalized

questions.explanation: Smallpox requires strict isolation of hospitalized patients (a highly contagious airborne/contact pathogen).

questions.stem: HPV vaccination to prevent cervical cancer is an example of:
questions.explanation: It's a vaccine → primary prevention, even though the ultimate target is a cancer.

questions.stem: The population pyramid of a mature population is characterized by:

options.A: Expansion of the base of the pyramid
options.B: Decrease in average age of marriage
options.C: Increase in average age of marriage
options.D: Decreasing mortality followed gradually by a sharp fall in fertility

questions.explanation: A mature population shows falling mortality then a marked fall in fertility → narrowing base, ageing structure.

questions.stem: Nitrosamine, produced from tobacco combustion, is considered a substance that:

options.C: Is the main cause of myocardial infarction

questions.explanation: Nitrosamines are carcinogenic.

questions.stem: Smoking damage to the cardiovascular system contributes to:
questions.explanation: Smoking → coronary heart disease / angina (via thrombosis & vasoconstriction).

questions.stem: Causes / risk factors for alcohol use include all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: The recognised causes are genetic, psychological and emotional. Comorbidities are coexisting conditions, treated here as the exception.

questions.stem: Screening programs, regarding prevention, are used for:

options.A: Early diagnosis of disease at a preclinical stage
options.B: Encouraging individuals to adopt healthy habits

questions.explanation: Screening = secondary prevention = early detection in the preclinical (asymptomatic) stage.

questions.stem: Which is NOT used for understanding the system when improving quality of health services?
questions.explanation: Fishbone, observation and surveys help map/understand the system; a run chart is used to track change/variation over time (the measurement phase) rather than to understand the system itself.

questions.stem: What is true about active immunity?

options.A: The host develops antibodies after vaccination or illness
options.C: Only when the host develops antibodies due to illness
options.D: When ready-made antibodies are administered for immediate protection

questions.explanation: Active immunity = the host produces its own antibodies after infection or vaccination. Ready-made antibodies = passive immunity.

questions.stem: WHO's goal: reduce the life-expectancy gap between socioeconomic groups by at least:
questions.explanation: At least 25%.

questions.stem: In the USA, one fifth of fatal road accidents involve:
questions.explanation: About one fifth of fatal crashes involve teenagers/adolescents.

questions.stem: Regarding studies on diet–cancer correlation, which is true?

options.B: Antioxidants are associated with a partial reduction of ALL cancer types
options.C: Reduced appearance of certain cancers from increased fruit consumption
options.D: Reduced appearance of all cancers from increased vegetable consumption

questions.explanation: Increased fruit intake is linked to reduced incidence of certain cancer types — not "all" cancers.

questions.stem: Modifiable risk factors for Coronary Heart Disease are:
questions.explanation: Dyslipidemia is modifiable; gender & genetics are not; low BMI isn't a CHD risk factor.

questions.stem: WHO's goal: reduce the life-expectancy gap among socioeconomic groups by at least:
questions.explanation: At least 25%.

questions.stem: Per WHO, the most common conditions contributing to DALYs include all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Top DALY contributors include IHD, stroke, road injury and COPD; colon cancer is not among the very top DALY causes.

questions.stem: In Greece, family planning was first institutionalized:
An advantage of a prospective cohort study is that:

options.B: All confounding factors can be calculated
options.C: Only proportion ratios can be calculated
options.D: Only relative risks can be calculated

questions.explanation: Cohort studies measure incidence.

questions.stem: Cigarette smoke is classified into:
questions.explanation: Tobacco smoke = mainstream + sidestream.

questions.stem: Road traffic accidents show:
questions.explanation: The canonical answer is local clustering and temporal periodicity (though summer increase & provincial-road frequency are also true features).

questions.stem: Modifiable risk factors for CHD include:
questions.explanation: Dyslipidemia is the modifiable factor.

questions.stem: Psychology of change (improving healthcare quality) — which is true?

options.A: In a few groups ~50% delay adopting an improved idea
options.C: Improvement is achieved only when the entire population adopts
options.D: Adoption of an improved idea rarely meets resistance

questions.explanation: All three specific statements misrepresent Rogers' curve → all are incorrect (resisters ≈16%, full adoption not required, innovation does meet resistance).

questions.stem: Which is true about Health Education?

options.A: It primarily targets community health improvement
options.B: It includes all opportunities contributing to individual and community health
options.D: It is an integral and dynamic tool of Health Promotion

questions.explanation: Health education is an indispensable / integral tool of Health Promotion (learning is very much consciously constructed, so option C is false).

questions.stem: The types of hosts / reservoirs are:

options.B: Four: human, animals, environment and microbes

questions.explanation: Human, animal, environmental — three.

questions.stem: What is herd immunity?

options.A: Disease transmission at the population level
options.B: Administration of antibiotics to the entire population
options.C: Susceptibility or resistance of a population to a specific disease

questions.explanation: Herd immunity = a population's resistance (or susceptibility) to a specific disease when enough members are immune to break transmission.

questions.stem: Smoking cessation, weight control, balanced diet and exercise in a woman of reproductive age with a history of gestational diabetes are examples of:
questions.explanation: Reducing risk factors before disease onset = primary prevention.

questions.stem: Non-modifiable risk factors for CHD include:
questions.explanation: Gender is non-modifiable; smoking & dyslipidemia are modifiable; low BMI isn't a CHD risk factor.

questions.stem: Diphtheria incidence is highest in:
questions.explanation: Diphtheria (respiratory droplet spread) peaks in the colder winter months.

questions.stem: Those more prone to accidents are:
questions.explanation: Men — roughly twice as many males as females die from injuries.

questions.stem: Causes contributing to alcohol addiction include all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Recognised causes are genetic, psychological and emotional; financial poverty is the exception here.

questions.stem: The statistical model for estimating CHD risk (SCORE) includes:
questions.explanation: SCORE includes arterial hypertension (systolic BP), with age, sex, smoking, cholesterol.

questions.stem: In direct transmission of infectious diseases:

options.A: Host and susceptible host are more than 1.5 m apart
options.B: Host and susceptible host are less than 1.5 m apart
options.C: Transmission occurs only through person-to-person contact

questions.explanation: Direct droplet transmission occurs at < 1.5 m.

questions.stem: Per Rogers' curve on the psychology of change during new quality interventions, which is true?

options.C: Everyone reacts the same way to change
options.D: A small percentage (16%) stubbornly resist and may never adopt (laggards)

questions.explanation: Laggards ≈ 16% resist and may never adopt. Early adopters are ~13.5%, and people clearly differ in their response.

questions.stem: The second phase of demographic transition is characterized by:

options.C: High birth rate and declining death rate
options.D: Slow decline in birth rate with significant decline in death rate

questions.explanation: Phase 2 = high birth rate, declining death rate.

questions.stem: Carcinogens are identified by all of the following EXCEPT:

options.A: Direct application of known carcinogens to volunteers and observation
options.D: Observing individuals exposed to carcinogens during treatments (e.g. radiation)

questions.explanation: You cannot ethically apply carcinogens to volunteers. Identification uses animal studies, occupational cohorts and observation of exposed patients.

questions.stem: All of the following are true EXCEPT:

options.A: Early colon cancer diagnosis gives good 5-year survival (90%)
options.B: Ovarian cancer causes the fewest reproductive-system cancers in women

questions.explanation: The ovarian cancer statement is the false one (it is neither the "fewest" nor benign — ovarian cancer has notably poor survival).

questions.stem: Regarding the "Dutch Hunger Winter" study, which is correct?

options.A: Only the early stages of pregnancy affect fetal growth trajectory
options.B: Fetal growth trajectory is determined in the first half of pregnancy
options.D: Early-pregnancy nutritional deficiency gave long-term advantages (greater resilience)

questions.explanation: The Dutch Hunger Winter showed early-gestation famine → long-term harm (obesity, metabolic/CVD disease, schizophrenia risk), and growth is not fixed only in the first half → so none of the above.

questions.stem: Nitrogen oxides in cigarette-smoke gases cause damage to:
questions.explanation: Nitrogen oxides damage the respiratory system.

questions.stem: The occurrence of an infectious disease requires:

options.B: The absence of a susceptible host
options.C: The presence of a causative agent

questions.explanation: A causative agent must be present (with a susceptible host and transmission).

questions.stem: A prerequisite for a confounding factor is that:

options.A: It must be related to the studied exposure
options.B: It must not be related to the exposure
options.C: It must not be a risk factor for the outcome
options.D: It must be an intermediate factor between exposure and outcome

questions.explanation: A confounder must be associated with the exposure, be an independent risk factor for the outcome, and not lie on the causal pathway.

questions.stem: Regarding prolonged starvation, which is true?

options.A: Reduced mental function, but for a shorter duration

questions.explanation: Prolonged starvation → reduced mental function, poor mental-health symptoms and reduced libido → all of the above.

questions.stem: In overviews of systematic reviews (umbrella reviews):

options.A: Their synthesis unit is only individual studies
options.C: Their synthesis unit is drug side effects
options.D: Their synthesis unit is systematic reviews

questions.explanation: Umbrella reviews synthesise systematic reviews (reviews of reviews).

questions.stem: A country's birth rate is influenced by:

options.B: The proportion of females in the general population
options.D: The proportion of males in the general population

questions.explanation: Birth rate depends on population composition — its age/sex structure, especially the share of women of reproductive age.

questions.stem: Hepatitis D is NOT endemic in:

options.B: Drug users and frequently transfused patients

questions.explanation: HDV is bloodborne (requires HBV co-infection) — not waterborne, so it is not linked to water scarcity (that fits hepatitis A/E).

questions.stem: The main causes of injury and death in aviation accidents are:

options.A: Fire and deceleration of the aircraft
options.C: Leakage of fuel and toxic gases

questions.explanation: The main cause is leakage of fuel and toxic/poisonous gases.

questions.stem: Road traffic accidents show:

options.D: An increase during the summer months

questions.explanation: The canonical feature is local clustering and temporal periodicity (they also rise in summer and on provincial roads).

questions.stem: Epidemiological surveillance of chickenpox (varicella) includes:

options.A: Strict isolation when the patient is hospitalized
options.B: Disinfection of patients' faeces and urine
options.C: Chemoprophylaxis of personnel during an outbreak

questions.explanation: Varicella (airborne/contact) requires strict isolation during hospitalization until all lesions have crusted.

questions.stem: Rubella is transmitted:

options.C: By direct contact with sufferers through droplets
options.D: After injury with a contaminated object

questions.explanation: Rubella spreads by direct droplet contact (respiratory route).

questions.stem: In secondary prevention of breast cancer, all are true EXCEPT:

options.B: MRI in women with a family history

questions.explanation: Prophylactic mastectomy = primary prevention, not secondary.

questions.stem: Among environmental factors for cancer, all are included EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Exercise is protective, not a risk factor.

questions.stem: Regarding quality improvement in healthcare, which is NOT true?

options.A: Adoption of necessary changes to improve therapeutic outcomes
options.B: Continuous and combined efforts of all involved are necessary
options.D: The processes run parallel to and connected with daily practice

questions.explanation: Options A, B and D are all valid QI principles, so "All" is the key here (every statement holds).

questions.stem: Regarding understanding the system (in quality improvement), what is true?

options.A: Understanding is the final step of the improvement effort
options.B: The ideal way comes from combining methods of inquiry

questions.explanation: Combining methods and brainstorming both hold, and understanding is not the "final" step → all are correct among the valid statements.

questions.stem: Regarding Health Promotion:

options.B: It covers a wide range of social & environmental interventions
options.C: It aims at adopting healthy attitudes and behaviours
options.D: It refers to enhancing people's ability to control their own health

questions.explanation: All three statements correctly describe health promotion → all are correct.

questions.stem: Regarding the goals of Health Education:

options.A: They are grouped into 7 basic categories
options.D: They are not always specific, as long as they include awareness, knowledge and value prioritization

questions.explanation: Health-education goals presuppose decision-making and behaviour change.

questions.stem: Regarding the results of the "Seven Countries" study, which holds?

options.A: The Mediterranean Diet is UNESCO-protected heritage
options.B: Longevity is due to genotype and is minimally affected by diet
options.D: The observations confirmed the definition of the Mediterranean Diet model

questions.explanation: The Seven Countries Study (Ancel Keys) confirmed/defined the Mediterranean Diet model as protective against CVD.

questions.stem: Which is true about prolonged starvation?

options.A: It reduces mental function but for a shorter period
options.B: It leads to symptoms of poor mental health

questions.explanation: Prolonged starvation → poor mental health + reduced libido + impaired mental function → all of them.

questions.stem: Regarding diet–cancer correlation studies, which is true?

options.B: Antioxidants partially reduce the prevalence of ALL cancers
options.C: Reduced appearance of certain cancers from increased fruit consumption
options.D: Reduced appearance of all cancers from increased vegetable consumption

questions.explanation: Increased fruit intake → reduced appearance of certain cancer types (not "all").

questions.stem: The summary result obtained after a meta-analysis is:

options.A: A weighted mean where smaller studies get more weight
options.C: A weighted mean where larger studies get more weight
options.D: A simple mean of the results

questions.explanation: In meta-analysis, larger studies are weighted more heavily.

questions.stem: Which is an advantage of a prospective cohort study?

options.B: It can calculate all confounding factors
options.C: It can calculate only proportion ratios
options.D: It can calculate only relative risks

questions.explanation: A cohort measures incidence.

questions.stem: How are nested case-control studies designed?
questions.explanation: Nested case-control studies are designed within a cohort.

questions.stem: WHO's goal: the life-expectancy gap between socioeconomic groups should be reduced by at least:
questions.explanation: At least 25%.

questions.stem: In Greece, family planning was first institutionalized:
In medical demography, the term "births" corresponds only to:
questions.explanation: "Births" = live births.

questions.stem: The population pyramid of a mature population is characterized by:

options.A: A decrease in average age of marriage
options.B: Expansion of the base of the pyramid
options.C: Decreasing mortality, followed gradually by a sharp fall in fertility
options.D: An increase in average age of marriage

questions.explanation: Mature population: falling mortality → then a sharp fall in fertility.

questions.stem: Diseases associated with alcohol include:
questions.explanation: Alcohol is causally linked to liver disease (and 200+ other conditions).

questions.stem: Causes / risk factors for alcohol use include all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Recognised causes are genetic, psychological and emotional; comorbidities are treated here as the exception.

questions.stem: Nitrosamine, produced from tobacco combustion, is considered a substance that:

options.C: Is the main cause of myocardial infarction

questions.explanation: Nitrosamines are carcinogenic.

questions.stem: The nitrogen oxides in cigarette-smoke gases cause damage to:
questions.explanation: Damage to the respiratory system.

questions.stem: The statistical model for estimating CVD risk (SCORE) includes:
questions.explanation: SCORE includes arterial hypertension (with age, sex, smoking, cholesterol).

questions.stem: Modifiable risk factors for coronary heart disease are:
questions.explanation: Dyslipidemia is modifiable; heredity & gender are not; low BMI isn't a CHD risk factor.

questions.stem: What is herd immunity?

options.A: Transmission of disease at the population level
options.B: The taking of antibiotics by the whole population
options.C: The susceptibility or resistance of a population to a specific disease

questions.explanation: Herd immunity = a population's resistance/susceptibility to a disease; enough immune members break transmission (R<1).

questions.stem: The types of reservoirs are:

options.B: Four: human, animals, environment and microbes

questions.explanation: Human, animal, environmental — three.

questions.stem: WHO goals for the 21st century are all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Increasing fertility is not a WHO goal.

questions.stem: Healthy life expectancy is an indicator that answers questions such as:

options.A: Which risk factor is the most dangerous?
options.B: Does improving health come with longevity?
options.C: At which prevention level can we intervene?
options.D: Which disease is responsible for deaths at a given moment?

questions.explanation: HALE addresses whether improving health is accompanied by longevity (years lived in good health).
